category: Large Polyp Stony (LPS) Coral — large single-polyp free-living; family Lobophylliidae

nativeRange.habitat: Sandy inter-reef areas and rubble zones at moderate to deeper depths; generally found resting free-living on soft substrates rather than attached to hard reef crests

decorPreferences: sand bed placement — essential for free-living polyp
smooth stable substrate — avoid sharp rockwork that can tear fleshy tissue
lower tank area with minimal flow
spacing of at least 15 cm from neighboring corals to allow full nighttime expansion

diet.notes: Derives nutrition from symbiotic zooxanthellae (photosynthesis) and benefits from regular supplemental target-feeding. Weekly feedings of meaty foods — mysis shrimp, krill, small pieces of marine fish, chopped seafood, or LPS pellets — have been reported to enhance coloration and growth. The polyp has a large gape and can accept surprisingly large food items. Feed in the evening when the polyp is fully expanded; use a pipette or turkey baster to direct food onto the oral disc.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals with adequate spacing
other Acanthophyllia specimens with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals (torch, galaxea, hammer, frogspawn) without generous spacing
predatory fish that may nip at fleshy polyps (triggers, pufferfish, large wrasses)
high-flow areas that prevent full polyp inflation
sharp rockwork that could abrade or puncture tissue

imageCandidates.notes: Oceanamazon; 3715x2672. Tack-sharp actinic/blue-lit in-tank shot of a single free-living polyp with radial orange oral disc on electric-green fleshy tissue — the fluorescent look hobbyists buy; diagnostic single-polyp meat/doughnut form clearly shown.

Oceanamazon; 770x578. Rainbow blue/orange color morph under blue reef lighting on a frag rack; sharp and vivid, shows full inflated single polyp. Lower resolution, kept as non-recommended variety shot.

summary: The Acanthophyllia Coral (Acanthophyllia deshayesiana) is a large single-polyp LPS coral prized for its vivid coloration and dramatic fleshy inflation. Generally considered intermediate in care, it is best placed on a sand bed in low flow and benefits from weekly target-feeding with meaty foods.
careNotes:
The Acanthophyllia Coral is the only species in genus Acanthophyllia and is among the most visually striking free-living LPS corals available to reef keepers. Its single large polyp can expand dramatically — several times its contracted size — displaying vivid coloration across a wide range of color morphs including blood red, electric green, orange, blue, and rare patterned forms.

Key care notes:

• Placement: The coral is free-living (not attached to substrate) and should be placed on the open sand bed in the lower third of the aquarium. Its natural habitat is sandy inter-reef areas and rubble zones. Placing it on rockwork is generally not advised — the fleshy tissue is delicate and the sharp calcareous skeleton can abrade soft tissue on rough surfaces. A minimum sand depth of about 5 cm is recommended to cushion the base and allow natural settling.

• Lighting: Generally thrives under low to moderate PAR (50–120). Acclimate new specimens gradually over 2–4 weeks.

• Water flow: Low to moderate indirect flow is strongly recommended. Direct or strong flow prevents the polyp from fully inflating, stresses the tissue, and can expose the skeleton. When the polyp remains contracted or deflated most of the day, flow is likely too strong.

• Depth note: Wild specimens have been collected from depths of 16–75 m, making this a relatively deep-water species by reef standards. Aquarium specimens generally adapt to reef lighting levels when acclimated carefully, but the preference for lower light in captivity reflects this deep-water origin.

• Aggression: Generally considered peaceful for an LPS coral. The polyp has been noted to extend feeding tentacles at night that can contact neighboring corals. Maintain at least 15 cm (6 inches) of clearance from neighboring corals. Multiple Acanthophyllia can reportedly be kept in proximity with adequate spacing.

• Feeding: Weekly target-feeding with meaty foods has been reported to improve coloration and growth rate. The large gape allows consumption of whole mysis shrimp, krill, or pieces of fish. Avoid overfeeding; remove uneaten food to maintain water quality.

• Skeleton safety: The calcareous skeleton is extremely sharp. Handle only when the polyp is deflated and support from underneath — contact with fingers can damage exposed tissue, and cuts from the skeleton are common.

• Fragging: Cannot be fragged due to the single-polyp structure. Captive sexual reproduction has not been accomplished commercially; wild-collected specimens dominate the trade. This, combined with vivid coloration, makes premium color morphs expensive ($200–$5,000+ for rare color forms).

• Trade name and taxonomic confusion: Acanthophyllia deshayesiana, Cynarina lacrymalis (the Australian Doughnut Coral), and Scolymia/Homophyllia species are all sold under the trade names 'Meat Coral' and 'Doughnut Coral', causing persistent confusion in the hobby. Key distinctions: Acanthophyllia tends to be the broadest, most uniformly fleshy, and largest of the three; Cynarina typically has a more inflated, vesiculated balloon-like polyp with distinct tissue zones; Scolymia/Homophyllia tends to be flatter. The older name 'Cynarina deshayesiana' is now treated as a synonym of Acanthophyllia deshayesiana, though some older sources conflated the two species; modern molecular studies support treating them as distinct taxa. Acanthophyllia deshayesiana should not be conflated with Cynarina lacrymalis, which is a distinct species with its own care profile and different visual characteristics.

• Hardy for LPS: Despite the premium price, Acanthophyllia is widely regarded among experienced hobbyists and specialty retailers as one of the more forgiving LPS corals in stable reef conditions, generally considered capable of tolerating moderate parameter variation better than SPS or more sensitive LPS corals such as the Elegance Coral.
breedingNotes: Acanthophyllia deshayesiana reproduces sexually via broadcast spawning in the wild (eggs and sperm released into the water column). Captive fragging is not feasible due to the solitary single-polyp structure; commercial-scale captive sexual reproduction has not been reported. Wild collection from Australia and Indonesia is the primary source for the aquarium trade.

primary.name: Tidal Gardens — Acanthophyllia Coral Care
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Provides PAR range (50–100), placement (sand bed, bottom of tank), flow (low to medium), kH (8–9 dKH), calcium (~425 ppm), magnesium (1350 ppm), nitrate (5–10 ppm), feeding foods (krill, silversides, LPS pellets, amino acids), care level (hardy, tolerates neglect), and colony behavior (non-aggressive with conspecifics). World Wide Corals liveaquaria page returned 404; Tidal Gardens used as primary per fallback rule.

additional.name: Extreme Corals — Care Requirements of Acanthophyllia
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 76–80°F (24–27°C), salinity 1.024–1.026 SG, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–10 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm. Agreed on low-moderate PAR (50–120), sand bed placement, low-moderate flow, weekly feeding with mysis/krill/chopped seafood. Scientific name confirmed as Acanthophyllia deshayesiana. Taxonomic family listed as generic 'LPS' — corrected to Lobophylliidae per WoRMS.

additional.name: Reef Builders — Three Solitary Showpiece Corals: Acanthophyllia, Indophyllia, and Cynarina
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 75–80°F (24–27°C), salinity 1.025–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–9 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm, PAR 50–150. Key source for Acanthophyllia vs. Cynarina vs. Indophyllia morphological and trade-name confusion discussion. Confirms Acanthophyllia as 'broadest, most uniformly fleshy' of the three. Feeding: 1–2 times per week.

additional.name: WoRMS — Acanthophyllia deshayesiana (Michelin, 1850)
additional.notes: Authoritative taxonomy: Family Lobophylliidae, Order Scleractinia. Synonyms include Cynarina deshayesiana and Protolobophyllia deshayesiana. Native range: Central Pacific, Coral Sea, Eastern Australia, Fiji, Great Barrier Reef, Philippines, Queensland, South China Sea, Sulu Sea, Western Pacific. Depth range: 16–75 m. Confirms Acanthophyllia deshayesiana as accepted name distinct from Cynarina lacrymalis.

additional.name: Frag Box Corals — Acanthophyllia Coral Care
additional.notes: Additional cross-check. Adult size up to 12 inches (30.5 cm) across; care level beginner/easy (averaged down to intermediate per playbook disagreement rule with other sources rating moderate). Notes on incompatibility with fragging, wild collection, price range ($500–$5,000+), and photosynthetic + supplemental feeding.
